subject: Accounting
chapter_name: Accounting for depreciation and disposal of non-current assets

note_data.title: Accounting for depreciation and disposal of non-current assets
keyPoints: Non-Current Assets are also known as Fixed Assets, these assets are acquired to earn revenue for the company and used for more than 12 months without being intended for resale
Examples of non-current assets include buildings and machinery, tools, and vehicles
Depreciation deals with the decrease in value of non-current assets over time
There are three methods of depreciation: Straight-Line Method, Reducing Balance Method, and Revaluation Method
The Straight-Line Method calculates depreciation evenly over the asset's useful life
The Reducing Balance Method applies a fixed percentage to the remaining book value each year
The Revaluation Method involves revaluing the asset at the end of each accounting period
Disposal of Assets involves removing assets from the books when they are sold, scrapped, or no longer used
An increase in an asset is a debit and a decrease is a credit

definitions.definition: Also known as Fixed Assets, these assets are acquired to earn revenue for the company and used for more than 12 months without being intended for resale
The resources owned and controlled by a company

Something you can touch, like a building, equipment, or furniture in an office. Tangible assets can be divided into two categories: inventory (things a company sells) and fixed assets (things a company uses to make its products or provide services)
Things a company owns that can't be touched or seen. They include things like patents, trademarks, brand recognition and goodwill
An asset which has a monetary value or can be converted into cash within 12 months

howToAnswer.instruction: Show all working clearly. Use the depreciation formula and show each step.
For disposal accounts, include all three elements: cost (Dr), accumulated depreciation (Cr), and proceeds (Cr)
Give the method + reason, e.g., 'Straight line method because it allocates equal amounts each year'

modelAnswer.question: A delivery van was purchased on 1 January 2020 for $24,000. It has a useful life of 4 years and a residual value of $4,000. The van was sold on 31 December 2022 for $8,500. Using the straight line method, calculate the profit or loss on disposal.
modelAnswer.answer:
Annual depreciation = ($24,000 - $4,000) ÷ 4 = $5,000
Accumulated depreciation (3 years) = $5,000 × 3 = $15,000
Net book value = $24,000 - $15,000 = $9,000
Proceeds = $8,500
Loss on disposal = $9,000 - $8,500 = $500

commonMistakes: Confusing current assets with non-current assets based on the 12-month rule
Incorrectly recording asset increases and decreases (forgetting that increase in asset is debit, decrease is credit)
Mixing up tangible and intangible asset classifications
examinerTips: Remember that non-current assets are used for more than 12 months without being intended for resale
Know the three methods of depreciation: Straight-Line Method, Reducing Balance Method, and Revaluation Method
formulas: Straight line depreciation = (Cost - Residual value) ÷ Useful life
Reducing balance depreciation = Net book value × Depreciation rate %
Net book value = Cost - Accumulated depreciation
Profit/Loss on disposal = Proceeds - Net book value
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: T-account showing Asset Disposal Account with cost debited on left side, accumulated depreciation and proceeds credited on right side, with balancing figure being profit or loss on disposal
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2022 P2 Q4 (3 marks) — they asked students to calculate the depreciation charge for the year ended 30 June 2022, requiring precise application of depreciation formulas

This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P2 Q5 (4 marks) — students had to analyze a machine disposal scenario with $120,000 cost, $12,000 residual value, 20% straight-line depreciation, testing disposal accounting entries
This topic was examined in M/J 2023 P1 Q8 (2 marks) — candidates were asked to explain why businesses should make provision for depreciation using the matching accounting concept
This topic featured in O/N 2024 P2 Q6 (5 marks) — students had to calculate motor vehicle depreciation charges totaling $10,320, testing systematic depreciation application

This topic was tested in M/J 2024 P1 Q12 (3 marks) — involved changing depreciation methods from straight-line to 25% reducing balance on an asset with $10,000 cost and $6,000 accumulated depreciation

workedExample.question: A machine was purchased on 1 January 2023 for $50,000. It has an estimated useful life of 5 years and a residual value of $5,000. The business uses straight-line depreciation. On 30 June 2025, the machine was sold for $22,000. Prepare the disposal account. (8 marks)
steps: Step 1: Calculate annual depreciation = (Cost - Residual Value) ÷ Useful Life = ($50,000 - $5,000) ÷ 5 = $9,000 per year
Step 2: Calculate total depreciation to disposal date = $9,000 × 2.5 years (Jan 2023 to June 2025) = $22,500
Step 3: Set up disposal account with original cost on debit side: Dr Disposal Account $50,000
Step 4: Transfer accumulated depreciation to credit side: Cr Disposal Account $22,500
Step 5: Record sale proceeds on credit side: Cr Disposal Account $22,000
Step 6: Calculate balancing figure: $50,000 - $22,500 - $22,000 = $5,500 loss on disposal
Step 7: Complete disposal account showing loss transferred to Income Statement
workedExample.answer: Disposal Account: Dr Cost $50,000, Cr Accumulated Depreciation $22,500, Cr Bank $22,000, Cr Loss on Disposal $5,500. The loss of $5,500 is transferred to the Income Statement as an expense.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct annual depreciation calculation, 1 mark for correct accumulated depreciation, 2 marks for proper disposal account format, 2 marks for correct entries, 1 mark for calculating loss, 1 mark for stating loss goes to Income Statement

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates failed to calculate part-year depreciation correctly when assets are bought or sold mid-year' — always calculate months/12 for partial years in depreciation questions

Examiner Report 2024: 'Students frequently confused the disposal account format, mixing up debits and credits' — remember cost and loss go on DEBIT side, accumulated depreciation and proceeds go on CREDIT side

Examiner Report 2022: 'Candidates often forgot to state where the profit or loss on disposal is recorded' — always mention that disposal gains/losses are transferred to the Income Statement as income/expense
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a T-account format Disposal Account showing: LEFT SIDE (Debits) - Original Cost of Asset, Profit on Disposal (if any); RIGHT SIDE (Credits) - Accumulated Depreciation to date of disposal, Sale Proceeds received, Loss on Disposal (if any). Include arrows showing transfers: accumulated depreciation FROM provision account, proceeds FROM bank account, and final profit/loss TO Income Statement. Label clearly with 'Dr' and 'Cr' and show the balancing process to find gain/loss.
note_data.quickRecap: Non-current assets are fixed assets used for more than 12 months to earn revenue without being intended for resale, including buildings, machinery, tools, and vehicles. Depreciation accounts for the decrease in value of these assets over time using three main methods. When assets are disposed of, they must be properly removed from the accounting records.
